description: Single-page React labyrinth app. Generates a maze inside a 500x800 area. Supports user input to draw a path from start to exit. Runnable locally via npm scripts.

userStories.title: Create empty React app scaffold
userStories.description: As a developer, I want an empty React app created in this repo so I can run and iterate locally.
acceptanceCriteria: Repository contains a React project scaffold created via standard tooling (CRA or Vite)
App runs locally with a single command (prefer: npm run start; acceptable: npm run dev if Vite, but add npm run start alias)
README includes exact commands for install and run (npm install; npm run start)
userStories.notes: Prefer Vite for simplicity; if Vite is used, ensure package.json has a 'start' script that maps to 'vite'.

userStories.title: Single-page UI layout with fixed maze area
userStories.description: As a user, I want a single page that contains a dedicated labyrinth area sized 500x800 so I can play consistently.
acceptanceCriteria: App is a single page (no routing required)
A visible container for the labyrinth is exactly 500px wide and 800px tall
Page shows a title and controls above the labyrinth area
Layout is responsive around the fixed labyrinth area (centered or aligned cleanly)
userStories.notes: Use CSS to enforce exact dimensions for the maze container.

userStories.title: Generate button and maze data model
userStories.description: As a user, I want a button to generate a new labyrinth so each game feels different.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Generate labyrinth' button exists and is clearly visible
Clicking the button creates a new maze (different layout most of the time)
Maze generation produces a data model (grid/cells/walls) stored in React state
Start and Exit positions are defined and rendered
start shiould be in top-left area, exit in bottom-right area, but not necessarily at corners and start should have at least 2 starting paths to prevent immediate dead-ends
userStories.notes: Use a standard maze algorithm (e.g., recursive backtracker). Keep the grid size reasonable for rendering performance.

userStories.title: Render labyrinth inside 500x500 area
userStories.description: As a user, I want to see the labyrinth walls clearly rendered inside the fixed area.
acceptanceCriteria: Maze is rendered fully within the 500x500 container (no overflow)
Walls are clearly visible; paths are navigable
Start cell and Exit cell are visually distinct (icons or colors)
Rendering approach is deterministic from the maze state (Canvas or SVG/HTML grid). row grid should be big
userStories.notes: Canvas is recommended for drawing and input tracking; SVG also acceptable. 1/10 of grid size

userStories.title: Input: draw a line from start to exit
userStories.description: As a user, I want to click/touch at the start and draw a continuous line through the labyrinth until I reach the exit.
acceptanceCriteria: Supports mouse input: pointer down, move, up to draw a line
Supports touch input (Pointer Events preferred) for drawing on mobile
Drawing starts only when user begins in the Start area/cell (or very close to it)
A drawn line is displayed in real-time as the user moves
A 'Clear path' action exists to reset drawing without regenerating the maze
userStories.notes: Use Pointer Events (pointerdown/move/up/cancel) to unify mouse + touch.

userStories.title: Collision rules: prevent drawing through walls
userStories.description: As a user, I want the game to prevent cheating by drawing through walls so the labyrinth is meaningful.
acceptanceCriteria: When the drawn path crosses a wall, drawing stops immediately at the last valid point (do not add the invalid point/segment).

After a collision, the user can resume drawing ONLY from the last valid point: the next pointerdown enables drawing and subsequent pointer moves extend the same path from lastValidPoint (no auto-reset to Start, no starting from arbitrary points).
Collision detection is reliable at typical pointer speeds.
User receives clear feedback on collision (visual indicator and/or short message).
userStories.notes: Rule A: resume strictly from lastValidPoint. Implementation hint: maintain lastValidPoint and pathPoints; on collision do not append invalid points; on next pointerdown set isDrawing=true and keep anchor=lastValidPoint; ignore attempts to start elsewhere.

userStories.title: Win condition and feedback
userStories.description: As a user, I want to know when I successfully reach the exit.
acceptanceCriteria: When the drawn path reaches the Exit area/cell, the app shows a success state (message/modal/banner)
Success state offers actions: Generate new labyrinth, or draw again
No false positives: must actually enter the Exit area via valid path (no wall crossing)
userStories.notes: Keep the UX minimal but clear.

userStories.title: Polish: README + basic quality checks
userStories.description: As a developer, I want clear run instructions and basic checks so the project is easy to use and maintain.
acceptanceCriteria: README includes: prerequisites (Node/npm), install, run, and short usage instructions
Project includes consistent formatting/linting (minimal setup is fine)
No critical console errors in dev mode
App builds successfully (if build script exists) or at least runs reliably via npm run start
userStories.notes: Do not over-engineer; keep dependencies minimal.

userStories.title: user stops drawing then cannot continue from where left off
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to ensure that if the user stops drawing (pointerup) and then tries to resume drawing from the last valid point (pointerdown near lastValidPoint), they can continue without the path resetting to Start or allowing arbitrary restarts.
acceptanceCriteria: After a pointerup event, the user can resume drawing by performing a pointerdown event near the last valid point (lastValidPoint) without the path resetting to Start.
The next pointerdown after stopping drawing should enable drawing and subsequent pointer moves should extend the same path from lastValidPoint.
The user should not be able to start drawing from arbitrary points on the maze; they must resume from/near lastValidPoint.
userStories.notes: Do not over-engineer; keep dependencies minimal.

userStories.title: Collision feedback causes layout shift
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to ensure that when a collision occurs and feedback is shown, it does not cause the labyrinth layout to shift or move down.
acceptanceCriteria: When a collision occurs and feedback is displayed, the labyrinth container remains fixed in place without any layout shifts.
Feedback is shown in a way that does not affect the positioning of the maze (e.g., overlay, fixed position element, or space reserved for messages).
userStories.notes: Use CSS to ensure feedback does not affect layout (e.g., position: absolute for messages).

userStories.title: Visual polish for Start and Exit icons
userStories.description: As a user, I want the Start and Exit positions to be visually distinct and engaging so I can easily identify them in the maze.
acceptanceCriteria: Start cell uses a distinct icon (Amelie PNG) that is clearly visible and appropriately sized for the maze cell.
Exit cell uses a different distinct icon (Serban PNG) that is clearly visible and appropriately sized for the maze cell.
Both icons are visually distinct from each other and from the maze walls.
Consider adding a simple animation or highlight effect to the start and exit icons to make them more visually engaging.
userStories.notes: Use CSS animations or simple JavaScript effects to enhance the visual appeal of the icons without overcomplicating the implementation.

userStories.title: Mobile responsiveness and touch optimization
userStories.description: As a mobile user, I want the labyrinth app to be responsive and optimized for touch input so I can play on my smartphone or tablet.
acceptanceCriteria: The labyrinth container scales appropriately on different screen sizes while maintaining the aspect ratio (e.g., using CSS max-width and max-height).
Touch input is fully supported for drawing paths, with no loss of functionality compared to desktop (using Pointer Events).
UI elements (buttons, messages) are easily tappable on smaller screens without causing layout issues.
userStories.notes: Test on various devices to ensure a good user experience across screen sizes.
